text: ARDNAMURCHAN, a parish partly in Argyle, and partly in Inverness-shire, formed into a peninsula by the bay of Kintra on the N, and Loch Sunart on the S. Its general aspect is mountainous, barren, and bleak. Pop. in 1821, 5,422; in 1841, 5,581.

ARDNAREE, a town in co. Sligo, on the r. bank of the Moy, which forms several fine cataracts in this neighbourhood. Pop. 1,699. Houses 325. The salmon and coast fisheries here are of considerable value.
ARDNURCHER, a parish partly in King's co., partly in co. Westmeath. Area 12,002 acres. Pop. 3,687.
ARDOCH, or Braco, a village in Perthshire, 8 m. N of Dunblane, near which are the remains of a celebrated Roman encampment.
ARDON, a village in the cant. of Valais, in Switzerland, on the Lucerne, near its junction with the Rhone. Its neighbourhood produces an excellent wine, resembling that of Champagne. Pop. 800.
ARDORE, a small town of Naples, in Calabria, Ultra Secunda, 7 m. S of Gierace. Pop. 2,000.
ARDOYE, a commune and town of Belgium, in W Flanders, 14 m. S of Bruges. Pop. 7,363.
ARDOYNE, a parish in co. Carlow. Area 9,477 acres. Pop. 1,791.
ARDPATRICK, a parish and village in co. Limerick. Area of the civil p. 624 acres; of the ecclesiastical p. 16,997 acres. Pop. of the former 101; of the latter, in 1831, 2,739.

ARDRA, or Ardrah, a territory of Africa, on the Slave coast. The country is well-cultivated, and fertile, producing Indian corn, millet, cotton, indigo, and fruits in profusion; salt-works are also established on the coast. The natives are a good-looking muscular race of men. They manufacture cloths both of cotton and grass with great ingenuity. The roads are good, and there are several rivers, by means of which the extent of inland navigation is considerable in this territory. Ardra was an independent ning of last cent., by the king of Dahomey, to whom it is now tributary.—The cap., of the same name, covers a great extent of ground, but is not populous in proportion. Adams estimated the pop. at from 7,000 to 10,000. It is situated 30 m. inland. The palace is described as spacious and magnificent. The city is surrounded by palm-trees, the oil of which forms a valuable article of commerce, and is also employed by the natives in the manufacture of soap.
ARDRAHAN, a parish in co. Galway. Area 17,948 acres. Pop. 4,191.
ARDREE, a parish in co. Kildare, on the Barrow, about 1 m. S of Athy. Area 323 acres. Pop. 205.

ARDRES, a cant., commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Saint-Omer. Pop. of cant., comprising 23 com., 13,911 in 1831; of com. in 1846, 2,193. The town, which is 14 m. W by N of Saint-Omer, is small but strongly fortified. It was in this neighbourhood that Francis I. and Henry held their celebrated interview in 1520. The plain on which they met still retains the name of Champ du Drap-d'or, or 'the Field of the Cloth of Gold,' in allusion to the magnificence of that pageant.
ARDRISHAIG, a village of Argyleshire, at the E entrance of the Crinan canal, 2 m. from Lochgilphead. Pop. 300.
ARDRISTAN, a parish in co. Carlow, 1½ m. SW of Tullow. Area 1,571 acres. Pop. 598.

ARDROSSAN, a parish and seaport town of Ayrshire, 1 m. N of Saltcoats, and connected by railway with Glasgow, from which it is distant 31 m. by railway route. Its safe and spacious harbour, constructed by the Earl of Eglinton, is enclosed by a pier 900 ft. long, and is accessible in all weathers. The pier is built upon a trap dyke; and the castle-hill has been formed by an eruption of trap. [Keir.] The export of coal from this place is very considerable; and steamers ply regularly between A. and Fleetwood on the Lancashire coast, making the voyage in from 12 to 14 hours. Pop. in 1821, 3,105; in 1831, 3,494; in 1841, 4,947.
ARDSALLA, a parish in co. Meath, on the r. bank of the Boyne. Area 1,739 acres. Pop. 341.
ARDSTINCHAR, or STINCHAR, a river of Ayrshire, which rises in the moors of Carrick, and falls into the sea at Ballantrae, after a course of 30 m. There is a good salmon-fishery on this stream.

ARDSTRAW, a parish and village in co. Tyrone, 6 m. from Strabane. Area 44,974 acres. Pop. 17,384. Houses 3,085. The v. of Ardstraw-bridge is situated on the Derg river, 3 m. NW of Newton Stewart. Pop. 134.
ARDSKEAGH, a parish in co. Cork, 2 m. S by E of Charleville. Area 1,929 acres. Pop. 280.
ARDTREA, a parish in co. Londonderry, and partly in co. Tyrone, extending along Lough Neagh and Lough Beg. Area 23,247 acres. Pop. 13,046.
ARDWICK, a chapelry in the parish of Manchester, in Lancashire, forming part of the parliamentary burgh of Manchester. Area 470 acres. Pop. in 1841, 9,906.
AREAS (SANTA ANNA DAS), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio Janeiro, on the Serra do Mar.
AREB, or Arrab, the name of two villages in Nubia, on opposite sides of the Nile, 10 m. N of Derr.

AREBO, or Arbon, a town on the Slave coast of Guinea, about 60 m. from the mouth of the Formosa, in N lat. 5° 58'. The Dutch have a factory here. The town is large and populous, and affords good facilities to the trade of the country.

ARECHAVALETA, a town of Spain, in Biscay, 2 m. S of Mondragon, on the Deba. It has manufactories of locks and of coarse cloth; and there are mineral springs in the vicinity. Pop. 800. ARE Lanzarote, a little to the S of the small but secure harbour of Naos, which forms its port. Pop. 2,500. -Also a station in the Pampas, 120 m. NW of Buenos Ayres, on the river Arecifé.—Also a town on the E coast of the island of Lancerota. Pop. 2,500.
ARECIVO, a town in the island of Porto-Rico, 28 m. from San Juan, in N lat. 18° 29', W long. 66° 39', on a stream falling into A. bay.
ARECO, a village of South America, on a river of the same name, 72 m. W of Buenos Ayres.
ARECUSA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 10 m. NW of Jeddo.

ARED (EL), or EL AAREDH, a district of Arabia, near the centre of the territory of Nejed, and containing the cap. of that division of Arabia, Deraiyah or Deraiah. The caravan-routes from Hajar or the W shores of the Persian gulf, to Mecca, cross this district, and pursue a SW course along the foot of a range of mountains called Jebel Ared or Jebel Imarych. On the W it has the district of El Woschem; on the N, El Sedeir; and on the SE, El Khardj.
AREGH, or ARRACK, a town of Bejapur, in Hindostan, 10 m. E of Merritch.
AREGNO, a commune and village of Corsica, about 8 m. from Calvi. Pop. 696.
AREKATSA, a river of Guayana, falling into the Parima from the NW, in N lat. 3° 44'.

AREMBERG, a duchy of Hanover, on the Dutch frontier, comprising the sovereignty of Meppen, ceded by Prussia to Hanover in 1815; the sovereignty of Recklinghausen; another sovereign domain in the Prussian territory of Munster, and extensive possessions in the Netherlands. Area of the Hanoverian territory 33.13 German or 710 English sq. m. Pop. in 1833, 41,546. Annual revenue about £70,000. The family of Aremberg were made princes by Maximilian II. in 1576, and sovereign-dukes by Ferdinand in 1644. By the peace of Luneville they were deprived of large possessions, but received in lieu a considerable portion of Westphalia. The duke lost his sovereignty in 1810, when Napoleon incorporated his dukedom with France and Berg. Since 1815, in consideration of some of his mediatized possessions, the duke has been created a peer of the Westphalian estates in Prussia; and, on account of other possessions, he is a member of the house of lords in Hanover. In 1826, the king of England elevated the duke's Hanoverian possessions to a dukedom, called Aremberg-Meppen. The duke is also a grandee of Spain, of the first class, and inherits large estates in France, and extensive tracts of forest in the Pyrenees. The ducal family are of the Catholic faith.— Also the cap. of the Hanoverian portion of the above, at the confluence of the Hase and Ems, 10 m. N of Lingen. Pop. 2,300.—Also a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle of Coblentz. Pop. 240.

ARENA, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra, 16 m. E of Nicotera. Pop. 1,790.—Also a river of Sicily, which enters the sea near Mazzara, in N lat. 37° 40'.—Also a village at the bottom of the bay of Howakil, in Abyssinia, in N lat. 15° 5'.
ARENAC, a county in the state of Michigan, U.S. It lies on the NW side of Saginaw bay, and is drained by the Rifle river and some small streams. Area 400 sq. m.
ARENALES, a remarkable district to the S of Paso, in New Mexico, consisting of a long and broad tract of dome-shaped hills of fine white sand.

ARENAS, an island in the mouth of the Orinoco, which is covered with water during high tides.- There are several other islands of this description, and bearing this name, on the South American coast.—Also a cape in Luzon, one of the Philippines, in N lat. 16° 15'.—Also a cape in the bay of Guate-

ARECIFE', a town on the E side of the island of | mala, in N lat. 11° 2', forming the E side of Port San Juan.—Also a cape near the mouth of the gulf of California, in N lat. 24° 0′, W long. 109° 35′.—Also a cape on the N coast of St. Christopher, one of the Caribbees, in N lat. 17° 27', W long. 63° 0'.—Also a cape on the E coast of Terra del Fuego, in S lat. 53° 28', W long. 68° 0'.—Also a cape on the gulf of Guayaquil, in N lat. 0° 30′, W long. 80°.

ARENAS (PUERTA DE), a pass of Spain, in the valley of the Campillos, in Granada, 18 m. from Jaen. The road and river together are here confined to a ravine, between perpendicular cliffs, not exceeding 60 ft. in breadth. On emerging from the pass, an open and cultivated valley presents itself, towards the head of which, and distant about 4 m., is the village of Campillos Arenas.

ARENDAL, a small town of Norway, in the bailiwick of Nedenaas, prov. of Christiansand, on a river of the same name, in N lat. 58° 25', E long. 9° 10'. It is built on piles, and the several parts of the town communicate by canals. It has a considerable export trade in timber, and in the vicinity are extensive iron-mines. Pop. in 1801, 1,698; in 1833, 2,090.

ARENDONK, a small town of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, 6 m. E of Turnhout, on the Wympe. It has manufactures of woollen, linen, and stockings, and some of the inhabitants support themselves by training hawks. Pop. 2,450.
ARENDSBERG. See ARNSBERG.

ARENDSEE, a town of Prussia, in the Old Mark of Brandenburg, on the confines of Hanover, near a small lake of the same name, 18 m. NW of Stendal. Pop. in 1827, 1,810. ARENEZ (SANTA MARIA DE), OF ARENS DEL MAR, a town of Spain, on the coast of Catalonia, 36 m. from Gerona. Manufactories of silk and cotton stockings, and other stuffs, are carried on here with considerable vigour. The vessels belonging to this town trade with various ports of Spain, Italy, and America. It contains an elegant parish church, a capuchin convent, a dockyard, and a naval school. Pop. 4,500.
ARENEZ-DE-MUNT, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 26 m. E by N of Barcelona, near the coast. Pop. 3,500.
ARENOE, a small island of Norway, in Finmark, in N lat. 70° 6', NE of Tromsoe.

ARENS, or Arensharde, a district of Denmark, in the duchy of Sleswick, through which runs the famous wall which Gottric, king of Denmark, built in the 9th cent., across the country from Hollingsted to Sley, as a defence against the inroads of the Saxons and Sclavi.

ARENSBURG, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Livonia, on the SE side of the island of Oesel, the cap. of a circle of the same name. It has a castle, a town-house, a public school, a Russian and a Lutheran church, and an hospital. It carries on a considerable trade in corn, timber, cattle, butter, cheese, tallow, and seals' fat and skins; its coast-fishery is also of some consequence. Pop. 1,500.—The district or circle of A. is composed of the islands of Oesel, Moon, and Runoe. The Lutheran is the prevailing religion.
ARENSDORF, the name of two villages of Prussia, one in the Middle mark of Brandenburg, the other in the mark of Priegnitz.
ARENSWALDE, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, 94 m. NE of Berlin. Pop. 3,000.
ARENTOS, or Arentis, the name of two small islands in the Eastern seas, in S lat. 5°, E long. 114° 56'.
ARENUSA, town of Naples, in Upper Calabria, 16 m. W of St. Severina, near the base of Monte Calistro.

AREQUIPA, an intendancy or prov. of Peru, 48 m. long, and 36 m. broad; bounded on the N by the intendancy of Lima; E by Bolivia; S by Arica; and W by the Pacific. It is watered by the Arequipa, the ARE Tambo, and the Chile. The climate is salubrious, and the soil fertile, producing wheat, sugar, maize, potatoes, cotton, flax, cochineal, coffee, strawberries, and other fruits of the temperate zone. There is a considerable quantity of wine made here, and exported to the adjacent provinces. Pop. 136,800.

AREQUIPA, the cap. of the above prov., is situated in the valley of Quilca, about 36 m. from the sea, and at an elevation of 7,775 ft. above its level, in S lat. 16° 16', W long. 72° 54'; 217 leagues SE of Lima; 60 SW of Cuzco; and 50 N of Arica. The river Chile, or the Rio del Volcan, flows through it. On the E rises the range of the Andes; to the NE the Volcan de Arequipa, whose summit towers 6,000 ft. above the adjacent ridge. In order to avoid the consequences of the frequent earthquakes to which it is liable, the houses are low, strongly built, and vaulted. It is the seat of a bishop, is divided into three parishes, and contains several convents, a college of Jesuits, a seminary, and an hospital. Manufactories of gold and silver cloth, and of woollen and cotton stuffs, are in operation here. A. carries on a considerable trade with Buenos Ayres, its exports being brandy, wine, flour, cotton, and sugar; and its imports, cattle, dried flesh, tallow, cocoa, &c. It also forms an entrepot for the merchandise of America and Europe; and the great commercial road from Lima to the southern provinces passes through the city. Mollendo is its port. The surrounding country is beautiful and fertile, and the climate is remarkably healthy and mild.—The volcano of A., a regular volcanic cone truncated at its summit, with its sister nevados, the Pichu-Pichu and the Chacani, two elongated serrated ridges, soars over the city in S lat. 16° 24'. The former has an alt. of 18,300 ft. above sea-level. Two leagues below the town, the Chile is joined by the Rio de Socovaya, and assumes the name of the Rio de Arequipa. A. was founded by Pizarro in 1536; it was devastated by earthquakes in 1582, 1600, 1604, and 1725.—Pentland.—Meyen.
ARES, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Teste-de-Buch, on the Basin d'Arcachon. Pop. 300.
ARETA, a district in the pashalik of Acre, in which are the ruins of the ancient Cæsarea, in N lat. 32° 20′, E long. 35° 30′.

ARETHUSA, a lake in the pash. of Kurdistan, formed by an expansion of the Tigris.—Also a celebrated fountain in the island of Ithaca, 6 m. from Vathi, in a deep ravine, 450 ft. above sea-level, midway between the shore and a cliff which closes the ravine at the distance of a mile from the sea. Burgess describes it as "a small basin, supplied by the constant dripping of water from the vault;" a writer in the United Service Journal [1831, vol. iii., p. 64.] as rising from below, and gently oozing through an arch of red stone into a wide stone trough, whence it meanders in graceful curves down the ravine. There is considerable reason to believe that this is the fountain celebrated by Homer, and that the precipice above it is the rock Corax.—Also a copious spring in the island of Sicily, rising in a grotto naturally arched with a roof of stone, over which the outer street of the city of Syracuse is carried, at a spot not further from the sea than 12 or 14 yards. The waters are exceedingly clear and fresh, and so copious as to form a considerable rivulet.
ARETTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Aramitz. Pop. 2,139.
AREVALO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, 18 m. SE of Medina-del-Campo, at the confluence of the Arevalillo and the Adaja. Pop. 5,000.

AREZZO, anciently Aretium, a province, district, and city of Tuscany. The prov. has an area of 1,781 Tuscan sq. m., or 460,200 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 228,416. It is divided into 18 administrative districts which are subdivided into 49 communes, and comprise the cities of Arezzo, Chiusi, Cortona, Montepulciano, and Borgo-San-Sepolcro.—The city of A. is situated 34 m. SE of Florence, in a fertile plain watered by the Arno and the Chiana, between Citta-di-Castello and Florence. It is the see of a bishop, has two collegiate churches, fifteen parish-churches, and several religious houses; besides a secular college. Pop. 10,000. The magnificent loggie in the Piazza maggiore here, are considered the masterpiece of Vasari. The cathedral is a fine specimen of the Italian-Gothic. A. had gone almost entirely to decay before Cosmo de Medici took it under his protection. It was the birth-place of Mæcenas, of Guido or Guittone the musician, of Vasari the architect, of Leonardo and Pietro Aretino, of Redi, and Pignotti. It disputes with Incisa the honour of having also given birth to Petrarch.
ARFEUILLE-CHATAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Evaux. Pop. 1,004.
ARFEUILLES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of La Palisse. Pop. 3,370.
ARGA, a river of Navarre, rising in the Pyrenees, passing Pampeluna, and falling into the Aragon near Villafranca, after a course of about 79 m.

ARGANA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, the capital of a district of the same name, in the pash. of Diarbekir. It is situated on the side of a mountain, under a lofty peak of a ridge of the same name which runs in a NE and SW direction between the tributaries of the Euphrates on the W, and the waters of the Tigris on the E, and is approached by roads so dangerous that a false step would be attended with inevitable destruction. It contains 600 Mahommedan and Armenian families. At the distance of 10 m. to the N are extensive copper-mines. It is in N. lat. 38° 15′, E long. 39° 20′.
ARGANDA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, 12 m. SE from Madrid. The surrounding country abounds in orchards and olive-grounds. Pop. 1,500.
ARGANIL, a town of Beira, in Portugal, 24 m. E of Coimbra. Pop. 3,000.
ARGAO, a town of Zebu, one of the Philippines, in N lat. 10° 18′, E long. 123° 39′.
ARGAROSSA, a river of Savoy, which joins the Isere, 3 m. NW of Moutiers.
ARGASCH, a town of Russia, 64 m. WSW of Simbirsk, in N lat. 55°.
ARGAUM, or ARIGRAMA, a small village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Berar, 38 m. from Ellichpur. Near this place Sir Arthur Wellesley defeated Nagpur Rajah, in 1803.
ARGE, a river of Lithuania, which runs into the Nemonin, 4 m. NW of Wipe.—Also a town of Irak, in Persia, on the Tigris, 170 m. NW of Bassora.

ARGELE'S, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées. Area of arrond. 133,849 hectares. Pop. in 1846, 41,479. It comprises the 5 cantons of Argelès, Aucun, Lourdes, Luz, and Saint-Pé.—The cant. comprises 29 com.—The com. is on the Gave d'Azun, at an alt. of 1,542 ft. above sea-level, in a valley of the same name, about 8 m. in length, and from 1 to 3 m. in breadth, bounded on both sides by lofty mountains, and intersected by the Gave. At its head it branches into two ravines, one of which runs up to Cauterets, and the other ascends the main stream of the Gave to Luz and Gavarnie. Pop. in 1836, 1,420.—Also a cant. com. and town in the dep. of Pyrénées-Orientales, arrond. of Céret. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. of com. 1,478.
ARGELIERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Ginestas. Pop. 760.
ARGEN, a river of Upper Suabia, rising in the ARG Alps, 7 m. N of Constance, and entering the lake of that name S of Bregentz, near Langenargen.
ARGENCES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, 16 m. E of Caen. Pop. 1,174. It is celebrated for its honey.
ARGENS, a river of France, in the dep. of the Var, which, after a winding course of above 66 m., in which it passes Vidauban, Le Muy, and Roquebrunne, enters the Mediterranean near Frejus.
ARGENT, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Cher, on the Saudre, 36 m. N of Bourges. Pop. of cant. in 1831, 4,322. It comprises 4 com. Pop. of com. 1,262.
ARGENTA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, 20 m. SE from Ferrara, on the Po de Primaro. Pop. 3,000.

ARGENTAN, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Orne. The arrond. has an area of 186,695 hectares. Pop. in 1846, 111,515. It comprises the 11 cantons of Argentan, Briouze, Econché, Exmes, La Ferte-Fresnel, Gacé, Merlerault, Mortrée, Putanges, Trun, and Vimoutier.—The cant. comprises 13 com.—The commune and town had a pop. in 1846 of 5,611. The town is 30 m. NW of Alençon, pleasantly situated on an eminence in the midst of a large and fertile plain, on the banks of the Orne. It carries on a considerable trade in corn, linen, lawns, cottons, gauzes, hats, leather, and horses. Its principal manufactures are lace and linen, and it has some tan-houses and bleachfields. Mezeray, the historian, was born here.

ARGENTARA, anciently Scardus, a ridge of lofty mountains between Macedonia and Bulgaria, a spur of the Balkan chain. Most of them contain large rocks of transparent tale; and probably derive their name from this circumstance.

ARGENTARO (MONTE), or MONTE ERICOLO, a lofty and rocky peninsula in the maremma of Siena in Tuscany, which is joined to the main by two narrow and low isthmuses which run between the lagune of Orbitello and the coast, and form here a double bay. It is of calcareous formation, has an area of nearly 22 m. in circumf., and attains an alt. of 1,720 ft.

ARGENTAT, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, arrond. of Tulle. The cant. comprises 11 com. The com. and v. are on the r. bank of the Dordogne, 12 m. SE of Tulle. Pop. 3,120. Good wines, and 'vins de paille,' as they are called, are made here.
ARGENTEAU, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. and 8 m. NE of Liége, on the r. bank of the Meuse. Pop. 670.
ARGENTENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Ancy-le-Franc, on the Armançon. Pop. 220.
ARGENTERA (CAPE), a headland on the W coast of Sardinia, in N lat. 40° 46', E long. 8° 7'.

ARGENTEUIL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, arrond. of Versailles. The cant. comprises 11 com. The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Seine, 6 m. NW of Paris. Pop. 4,542. The celebrated Heloise took the veil in the convent of Argenteuil, and was prioress of the Benedictine nunnery which formerly stood here. The neighbourhood is rich in fruit, vegetables, and wine. It also contains quarries of gypsum, which is mostly transported to Normandy.- Also a village of France, in the dep. of Yonne, on the Armançon, 8 m. S of Tonnerre. The surrounding district is rich in corn, wine, and excellent pasture. Pop. 735.—Also a seignory in Lower Canada, in the county of the Two Mountains, on the Ottawa river.

ARGENTHAL, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, 40 m. E from Treves. ARGENTIE'RA, ARGENTIERE, OF KIMOLI, an- AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND ciently Cimolus, a small island in the Archipelago, near Milo, having a good roadstead on the SE, in N lat. 36° 48', E long. 24° 36½'. It is 5 m. long, and 3½ m. in breadth. Pop. about 800. Terra cimolita, a kind of chalk used in the bleaching of linen, is dug here. It is mountainous and unproductive, producing only a little cotton, corn, and fruit. The inhabitants occupy one village; and are said to make excellent pilots.

ARGENTIE'RE (L'), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, arrond. of Briançon, 29 m. NE of Gap, on the r. bank of the Durance. The cant., which has lead-mines and slate-quarries, comprises 7 com. Pop. of com. 1,196.
ARGENTIE'RE. See LARGENTIE'RE.

ARGENTIE'RE, or ARGENTINE, a town of Sardinia, on the Arc, 12 m. NW of St. Jean de Maurienne. Pop. 1,200. It has an iron forge and lead- Manufactures of silk and other articles mines. are here carried on with considerable activity.

ARGENTIE'RE (Col D'), a mountain-pass in the Upper Alps, in Piedmont, leading from Barcelonette in France, to Coni in Italy. It has an alt. of 7,200 ft. above sea-level. At Borgo San Dalmazio, 4 leagues from Demont, the route falls into the road which leads from the coast, over the Col de Tende, to Coni and Turin.

ARGENTINA, a town of Naples, in Calabria Citra, at the foot of the Apennines, 10 m. SW of Avignano.-Also a settlement on Blanco bay, on the N shore of the estuary of the Plata, in S lat. 38° 43' 50", W long. 62° 15'.
ARGENTINE REPUBLIC. See LA PLATA.

ARGENTON-CHATEAU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sèvres, arrond. of Bressuire. The town is on the summit of a steep hill, 12 m. W of Thouars. It was nearly ruined in the Vendée war. Its present pop. is 566. It has some manufactures of flannel and serges, and the wines of the neighbouring district are esteemed.

ARGENTON-SUR-CREUSE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre, arrond. of Chateauroux. The cant. comprises 10 com. The com. and town are 19 m. W of Chateauroux. Pop. 4,000. The town is divided into Upper and Lower, by the intersection of the river Creuse. In the Upper division are the ruins of a strong fortress, which was destroyed by Louis XIV.
ARGENTON-L'EGLISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Argenton-Chateau. Pop. 915.
ARGENTRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, 27 m. E of Rennes. Pop. 1,867.
ARGENTRE-SOUS-LAVAL, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, on the Jouane, 6 m. E of Laval. Pop. of com. 1,591. There are quarries of black and white marble here.
ARGHA, a town of Hindostan, in Nepaul, in N lat. 28° 15'. It contains about 450 houses.
ARGHANDI, a village of Afghanistan, in the Maidan valley, 20 m. SW of Cabul.
ARGHASAN, a river of Afghanistan, rising in the Amran mountains, and flowing W to the Turnak, which it joins in about N lat. 31° 31'. Its bed is dry a great part of the year.

ARGHURI, or Acorni, an ancient Armenian village upon Mount Ararat, which was totally destroyed, with its entire pop. of 1,000 souls, by the great earthquake and partial fall of the mountain in 1840. Parrot has given a description of this interesting place as it existed during his visit in 1829.
ARGIEH, a populous village of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Bagdad, on the Euphrates, 25 m. N of Sura.

ARGIRO-CASTRO, called by the Turks Erghile, born. In the morass of Argolis that hero slew the ARG a town of Albania, on the W side of the spacious valley of Deropuli, which is watered by the Argiro, or Ergir, a tributary to the Voyussa, which falls into the Adriatic. It is built upon a number of rocky acclivities, and has a well-fortified castle with extensive barracks which defends the important pass of Derbend. Pop. 4,000. Its ancient names were Phanole and Hadrianopolis. The vale of A. is about 30 m. in length, and 4 or 5 m. in width. A ridge about 3,000 ft. alt., looking with a bluff rugged face towards Corfu, but shelving down evenly towards the N and E, lies between it and Delvino. A ridge of equal height and extent, presenting a bold beetling face towards the valley, bounds its opposite side; and a third escarpment rises behind. These ridges are of mountain limestone interstratified with silex. Above the limestone appear sandstone and aluminous shale, decomposing readily into clay. The appearance of the country from the loftiest ridges, is that of an ocean, with the waves rolling after one another, and turned to limestone rock just as their threatening crests were about to break. Towards the W, the plains exhibit from below a perfectly levelled surface; and between the larger ranges, the aluminous formations, cast about in all directions, form domes and monticules of exquisite beauty.—Urquhart.
ARGIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Saint-Rambert. Pop. 593. There are silkmills here.

ARGISCH, a river of Germany, rising in the mountains which separate Wallachia from Transylvania, and entering the Danube near Mireni.—Also a town of Wallachia, on the above river, 50 m. SE of Hermannstadt.—Also a mountain which rises in a peak from the plain of Cæsarea, in the pash. of Angora, about 10 m. S of that town. See ARDIJ.

ARGITE, a small island in the gulf of Ægina, 16 m. E of Ægina, in N lat. 37° 42′, E long. 23° 54′. ARGJUN, ARGINA, or ARGA-TAU, a chain of mountains in Independent Tartary, NE of Kashgar, terminating near Tashkund on the river Sihun.

ARGO, an island of the Nile, in the kingdom of Dongola. It contains the ruins of an ancient temple, together with two colossal statues of red granite and remarkably fine workmanship.—Also a dangerous reef in the Fiji archipelago.
ARGOL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, 21 m. NW of Quimper. Pop. 1,132.

ARGOLIS, a department of modern Greece, in the Morea; bounded on the N by Corinthia; on the E by the Saronic gulf; on the S by the channel of Hydra, the gulf of Argolia, and the dep. of Cynuria; and on the W by Mantinea. Its area is 15.87 New Greek sq. m. = 621 English sq. m. Pop. in 1836, 26,720. On the W it is separated from Arcadia by a high range of mountains in which Monte Malevo attains a height of 5,757 ft. From the NW portion of this range a ridge runs through the dep. in a SE direction to its extreme SE point Cape Skillo. The Inachus is the principal river. The coast presents a very irregular outline, and is generally low. The only good harbour is that of Anapli or Napoli di Romania. Argolis is said to have been settled at an earlier period than any other part of Greece, and this opinion is, in some degree, supported by the architectural remains which are scattered over its territory. Most of these bear an Egyptian character, and the Cyclopean walls of Tiryns, in particular—which rise in solitary majesty from the central part of the plain -belong to the most remote antiquity. Here reigned Pelops, an emigrant from Asia Minor, from whom the peninsula derives its name. Here, too, was the seat of government of Atreus and Agamemnon, Adrastus, Eurystheus, and Diomedes; and here Hercules was Lernæan hydra; and, in the cave of Nemea, he subdued the ferocious lion. In the earliest times, Argolis was divided into the small kingdoms of Argos, Mycenæ, Tirynthus, Træzene, Hermione, and Epidaurus, which afterwards formed free states.

ARGONNE, a mountainous and forest district of France, which formed part of the ancient province of Lorraine. It comprehended the tract of country between the rivers Meuse, Marne, and Aisne; and may be described as a large forest interspersed with rude towns and villages. The inhabitants are principally engaged in felling timber, and herding cattle. It was bestowed by Louis upon the prince of Condé, and his successors used it only as a hunting-ground; but as it soon became the resort of smugglers, it was purchased by the Crown in 1782. During the French revolution this district was the scene of much bloodshed. The arrond, of Sainte-Menehould in the dep. of Marne, and some cantons in the dep. of Meuse and of Ardennes have been formed out of this territory.

ARGOS, a town in the Morea, in the dep. of Argolis, 22 m. S of Corinth, 41 m. N of Anapli. It stands upon the site of the ancient city of the same name, so celebrated in Grecian story; to the SW of the river Inachus or Zeria which rises in the mountains about 10 m. from A. It retains little or nothing of its former importance. The houses are ill-built, being principally constructed of earth; but are ranged in right lines, and are generally white-washed on the outside. During the late war it was twice or thrice besieged, and in 1825 was reduced to nearly complete desolation, though it is said to be again reviving. Most of the ancient edifices with which it was adorned have entirely disappeared. Among its ancient monuments is a theatre in the southern extremity of the town, partly hewn out of the solid rock by the Greeks, and partly constructed of bricks by Roman architects. The ancient citadel was situated on the heights of Larissa, which are now covered with the ruins of a modern fortress. These heights command a magnificent view of almost all Argolis, the bed of the Inachus, the Alcyonian lake, the plain of Lerna, the majestic summits of Arcadian Cyllene, and a great part of the Arcadian territory. [Clarke.] Its pop. was estimated by Clarke at 6,000, nearly all Greeks, or Albanian peasants; in 1829, Anderson estimated the pop. at not less than 8,000. The plain of A. is of remarkable beauty, "level as the still surface of a calm sea" [Clarke], studded with villages, and covered with corn, cotton, orange-trees, myrtles, and flowering shrubs; but Leake asserts that every part of the Argolic plain is unhealthy in summer, and that the heat is excessive. Under the late sway of the Porte, A. was an appanage of the sultan's sister, and governed by a waivode sent from Constantinople.—Clarke.— Dodwell.—Leake.

ARGO'STOL, or Argostoli, a seaport town of Cephalonia, the cap. of the island, 8 m. WSW of Cephalonia. Pop. 8,000. It is a modern place, having been built by the Venetians in the course of last cent.; but the houses are low and ill-built. It possesses, however, an excellent harbour, with dockyards, and its flotilla is one of the most numerous in the Archipelago. It contains also some cotton-spinning manufactories, and a pottery. The climate is unhealthy.—The gulf of A. has its entrance between Cape Aji, in N lat. 38° 8′ 40″, E long. 20° 23½, and Cape St. Nicolo, which are 45 m. distant from each other. Two-thirds from Cape N. is the small low isle of Guardiani. The inner harbour, on which the town is seated, is 11 league from Cape N. The rise and fall of the tide here is from 1 to 3 ft.
ARGOTA, a small port of Spain, on Bilbao bay, one-third of a mile within Punto San Ignacio.
ARGOUGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Saint-James. Pop. 1,542.
ARGOVIA. See AARGAU.
ARGUEDAS, a town of Navarre, 5 m. NW of Tudela, on the Ebro. ARG
ARGUEIL, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, 21 m. NE of Rouen. Pop. of com. 463.
ARGUELLO (POINT), a cape on the W coast of California, in N lat. 34° 30', W long. 120° 20'.
ARGUENON, a river of France, in the dep. of Côtes-du-Nord, which rises in the lake of Jugon, and falls into the sea at Guildo, 7 m. W of St. Malo, after a course of 30 m.
ARGUIDAH. See ACQUIDAH.

ARGUIN, or ARGUINN, an island on the coast of Africa, 10 leagues SE from Cape Blanco, in N lat. 20° 27', W long. 16° 37'. It is about 30 m. in length, and 1 in breadth; and lies 8 m. from the main. The N part is flat; the S. rises to an elevation of about 30 ft. It has no wood, but excellent water. It is supposed to be the ancient Cerne where Hanno planted a colony. It was fortified in 1664 by the Portuguese, and was for many years disputed between them, the Dutch, English, and French. It is now in the hands of the Arabs, who hold two fairs here in the months of June and December, when they exchange fish and oil for beads, cloths, and tobacco. The fish consist of mullet, bream, and herrings. - Grover.
ARGUN. See AMUR.

ARGUNSKOI, a town and fortress of Siberia, on the Argun, 160 m. from its junction with the Amur, at its confluence with the Kamara. Near it are mines of silver, and gold is also found in its neighbourhood.
ARGUT, a river rising on the Chinese frontier, and descending to the Katunia.
ARGY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre, arrond. of Chateauroux. Pop. 1,586.

ARGYLE, or ARGYLL, an extensive shire, on the western coast of Scotland, comprehending several large islands, as well as a considerable portion of the mainland. The latter part—which is of a very irregular figure—is bounded on the N by Invernessshire; on the E by the counties of Perth and Dumbarton, and the frith of Clyde; on the S and W by the Irish sea and the Atlantic ocean. According to Playfair, this co. lies between lat. 55° 15' and 56° 55' N; and long. 4° 32' and 6° 6' W; and extends 90 m. from N to S; and, in some places, upwards of 40 m. from W to E. Its area, according to the same authority, is about 2,400 sq. m., or 1,536,000 acres, exclusive of its islands. But this county is intersected by so many inlets of the sea, and has as yet been so imperfectly surveyed, that no correct estimate can be formed of its extent. Dr. Smith, in his Agricultural Survey of Argyleshire, estimates its length, from Loch Eil to the mull of Kintyre, at 115 m.; and its breadth from Ardnamurchan to the source of the Orchy, at 68 m. He also estimates the superficial area, exclusive of the islands, at 2,735 sq. m.; while Sir John Sinclair has calculated it at only 2,260 sq. m. The islands belonging to this shire have a joint superficial area, according to Smith, of 1,063 sq. m.; and according to Sinclair, of 929 sq. m.; making a total area, according to the former, of 3,798, and, according to the latter, of 3,189 sq. m., or 2,002,560 English acres, being one-tenth of the whole surface of Scotland. These admeasurements must be regarded of course as mere approximations to the actual area both of mainland and islands; nor until the trigonometrical survey of Scotland is published is it worth while to attempt their rectification from existing materials. The surface of this highly romantic region consists alternately of bleak barren moorlands, rugged chains of mountains, deep glens, winding inlets of the sea, and extensive sheets of inland water. The NE division is peculiarly bleak, rugged, and mountainous, but interspersed with narrow and sheltered glens; the W section is very irregular in its outline, and deeply indented by large bays, here called locks. The greater proportion of what may be called arable land is composed of the level tracts along the coasts. About oneseventh part of the surface is under cultivation. In general a light loam mixed with sand, on a bottom of clay or gravel, prevails; on the acclivities of the hills the most common soil is a light gravel on till; in the lower grounds, there is sometimes a mixture of clay and moss, and sometimes a coat of black mossy earth. The soil appropriated to pasture is partly dry, and partly wet and spongy; a considerable proportion of what is either flat or hilly is covered with heath. The summits of the highest hills are generally bare and barren rocks.-Limestone is found in every part of the county. In the island of Easdale, and at Ballahulish in Appin, are quarries of excellent blue slate. Marble exists in various quarters; and granite is quarried near Inverary. Veins of lead are frequent in the limestone and other strata; mines of this metal are wrought at Strontian, at Tyndrum, and in Isla; in the latter island copper and iron are also wrought, and copper has been found at Kilmartin on the main. The species of earth called strontites, or strontian, was first discovered at Strontian in the district of Ardnamurchan in 1791, and is still extensively wrought. Coal is wrought near Campbelton, and also in the island of Mull. Granite forms the great mountain-masses in the NE part of the county; but mica-slate predominates in the geological features both of the mainland and isles. An extensive tract of porphyry occurs on the N side of Loch Fyne; floetztrap prevails in a few districts.—The principal mountains are Ben-Cruachan, 3,669 ft.; Ben-More in Mull, Arthur, or the Cobbler, 2,389 ft.; Ben-More in Rum, streams are the Urchay or Orchy, and the Awe; the former flowing into, the latter flowing from, Loch Awe. Besides these, there are a multitude of minor streams, more distinguished by the romantic beauty of their course, than the volume of their water or their length.—Loch Awe is the principal inland lake. The total area of the fresh water lakes in Argyleshire is about 52,000 acres. The extent of marshy and mossy ground must be very great. Natural woods and plantations cover about 50,000 acres. The climate of this district is upon the whole mild, but excessively humid. In the NE quarter, where the general elevation is greatest, it is often very cold. The principal branch of rural industry is that of rearing cattle and sheep. The quantity of grain produced bears a small proportion to the area. Oats are the principal grain raised; but a large import of meal is required for the home-consumption. Potatoes are extensively cultivated, the poorest shieling having uniformly attached to it a small patch of potatoground. The cattle reared here are of a small size, but highly esteemed in the markets of the South, to which they are exported in immense numbers. The sheep are chiefly of the Linton or black-faced breed; and have on the mainland displaced the horned cattle in most farms. Red deer are still found in some of the forests; and grouse and ptarmigans are plentiful. The manufacturing industry of A. is unimportant. A large quantity of kelp used formerly to be manufactured along the shores, but it has been driven out of the market by foreign barilla. The fisheries, however, on the coast, and particularly in the lochs, are productive and improving. The two principal fishing-stations are Inverary and Campbelton; but considerable quantities of herrings are caught and cured at various stations along the coast, and on the shores of the different lochs. Some leather is manufactured; and coarse woollen yarns, stuffs, and stockings, are still made to a considerable extent. The establishment of steam-packets between various points on the coast of Argyle and along the shores of its lochs, and the larger towns on the frith of Clyde up to Glasgow, has given a great impulse to the industry of this district. There is not now a loch, bay, or inlet, but holds a daily, or at least commands a weekly, communication with the lowlands and the several districts of the country. Previous to the abolition of the feudal system, in 1745, the obstacles to improvement either in agriculture or manufactures were quite insuperable in this district of Scotland. The abolition of that system,—the conversion of corn-rents, or rents in kind and services, into money-rents, -the suppression of smuggling,-the execution of the Caledonian and Crinan canals,—the formation of excellent lines of road throughout the county under the auspices of the parliamentary commissioners,—the more general diffusion of education,—and the introduction of a system of farming better adapted to the character and capabilities of the soil and country,-have all contributed to the improvement of this interesting district. But the main impulse has undoubtedly been given to industry in this quarter of the country by the introduction of steam-navigation, and the reciprocal intercourse which has consequently taken place between all parts of Argyleshire and the manufacturing districts of the west of Scotland. The average rate of wages is from 1s. 6d. to 2s. a-day. Argyleshire is divided into six districts: viz.- Pop. in 1831. Pop. in 1841. Houses in 1841. 1. Argyle Proper, . The above pop. in 1831 composed 19,252 families, of whom 9,116 were engaged in agriculture; 3,241 in trade, manufactures, and handicraft; and 6,895 were not comprised in either of the preceding classes. The total pop. of the islands in that year amounted to 35,065; that of the mainland to 66,335. In 1841, the pop. on the mainland was 63,974; on the islands 33,397; and of this pop. 86,939 were natives of the shire. In 1801, the total pop. of this county was 71,859; in 1811, 85,585; in 1821, 97,316. It would thus appear that the rate of increase of population has been falling-off since the commencement of this century; and that during the ten years preceding 1831, it amounted to only 4 per cent., while the decennial ratio of increase on the population of England and Wales, since the commencement of the century, has been somewhat more than 16 per cent. The slow increase of population in this shire may be attributed partly to the limited nature of its territorial resources; partly to the extensive emigration which has long taken place from this county chiefly to Canada; and partly to the system so generally pursued by the large proprietors of throwing several small farms into the hands of one tenant, and discountenancing any attempt at minute subdivision of the soil.—The number of parishes in 1831 was 50, besides several mission-stations and chapelries. The synod of Argyle comprehends the presbyteries of Argyle, Dunoon, Kintyre, Islay, Jura, Lorn, and Mull. The number of parochial schools in 1834 was 70; and of schools not parochial 194. The total number of children at these schools was about 15,000. The Gaelic language still predominates here; but the English is almost universally understood by the natives. A number of islands are attached to this county, of which the chief are Muck, Tyree, Coll, Mull, Lismore, Islay, Jura, Colonsay, Staffa, Icolmkill, &c., which are severally described under their respective articles. The principal towns are Inverary, which is the county-town, Campbelton, and Oban. These three burghs unite with Ayr and Irvine, in Ayrshire, in returning one member to parliament; the county returns another. The parliamentary constituency in 1838 was 1,589; in 1842-3, 1,690. Argyle gives the title of Duke and Earl to the chief of the family of Campbell, one of the most powerful of the Scottish nobility. The county is mostly peopled with this clan; and its principal proprietors are of it. The valued rent of the county in 1674 was £149,595 10s. Scots; the real rent, as assessed in 1815, £227,493; in 1842-3, £262,273. Argyleshire is said to derive its name from Earra Ghaidheal, 'the country of the western Gael.' It was much infested, in ancient times, by predatory intruders, and has been in consequence the scene of numerous battles and heroic achievements. The deeds of Fingal and his heroes-if we may repose any confidence in the voice of Tradition-were mostly performed in this district; and numerous monuments of the remotest antiquity still remain to demonstrate the warlike spirit of its former inhabitants. In the middle ages the Macdougals of Lorn held sway over Argyle and Mull; while the Macdonalds, Lords of the Isles, were supreme in Islay, Kintyre, and the southern islands. These two chiefs were almost independent thanes, until their power was broken by James III., and by the transference of Lorn to the Stuart family by marriage. The erection of the earldom of Argyle in favour of Campbell of Loch Awe, in 1457, also greatly contributed to check the discords of the petty chieftains throughout this territory. The dukedom of Argyle was created in 1701.

ARGYLE, a township in Washington co., New- York, U. S., on the E bank of the Hudson, 45 m. from Albany. Pop. 3,111.—Also a township in the state of Maine, 89 m. NE of Augusta. Pop. 527.- Also a township in Shelburne county, Nova Scotia, lying along a bay of the same name, in which the Tusket isles, upwards of 300 in number, lie clustered. -Also a co. in New South Wales, about 60 m. in length, and 25 in breadth; bounded E and N by the Derwent, W by Glenorchy, and S by Queenborough. Towards Sydney it consists of patches of land of fine quality, surrounded by barren tracts, which gradually disappear towards the interior, and are succeeded by a series of extensive plains covered with fine pasturage, and varied by picturesque grassy hills. It is watered by tributaries of the Hawkesbury and Shoalhaven rivers. At its nearest point it is 25 m. distant from the sea.
ARGYRO-CASTRO. See Argiro-Castro.
ARHEILIGEN, a village and district in Hesse- Darmstadt, in the principality of Starkenburg, near Langen. Pop. 1,700.
ARIA, a town of Japan, in the island of Ximo, 18 m. SSE of Nangasaki.
ARIANAS, a district of Ecuador, in S lat. 1°.
ARIANCOPANG, a town of Hindostan, 3 m. from Pondicherry, in N lat. 11° 54'. This place was taken by Admiral Boscawen in 1748.

ARIANNA, a small village 6 m. NE of Tunis, remarkable for a fine fragment of the great Carthaginian aqueduct, 74 ft. high, supported by columns 16 ft. square, which increase in grandeur the nearer they approach the site of Carthage. Near this spot, several ancient matamores, or subterranean magazines for corn, capable of containing 100 bushels, strongly arched with large square stones, have been discovered.

ARIANO, a town of Naples, 15 m. NE of Benevento. It is perched upon a hill of tufa, in a very conspicuous situation, and many of the poorer ARI a bishop suffragan to the archbishop of Benevento. Besides the cathedral, there are 20 parishes and convents. This town has frequently been visited by earthquakes of a most appalling kind: the last happened in 1732. Cluvier places the ancient town of Equus Tuticus at this spot.—Also a town in the territories of the Pope, situated on a branch of the Po, 24 m. NE of Ferrara. Pop. 2,289.
ARIARI, a river of South America, in Granada, which, after a winding course, falls into the Guaviare, in N lat. 3° 30'.
ARIAS, a river of Buenos Ayres, which falls into the Plata, in S lat. 24° 35'.

ARICA, a prov. of Peru; bounded on the N by the prov. of Moquehua; on the E by Recajes; on the S by the province of Atacamas; on the W by the Pacific; and on the NW by Arequipa. It lies between lat. 18° and 19° S. The high lands of this district are dry and barren; the valleys yield wheat, maize, olives, &c. Pepper, cotton, and sugar are also cultivated; and in the mountains towards the Andes many cattle and sheep are reared.

ARICA, the cap. of the above province, is situated on the coast of the Pacific, and has a convenient harbour, 907 m. sailing distance from Valparaiso; 709 from Coquimbo; 612 from Huasco; 579 from Callao; and 108 from Iquique. Although it has suffered considerably from earthquakes, it is still a place of some trade; olives, cotton, sugar, and pepper, being its principal articles of commerce. Goods destined for Bolivia and Peru are landed here. In 1680, it was plundered by the British, when many of the inhabitants retired to Tacna, 36 m. distant. It has been nearly abandoned by the whites on account of its unhealthiness; and contains only about 150 families, chiefly blacks, mulattos, and Indians. About a mile from the town is an ancient cemetery, on the side of a hill, in which the bodies appear to have undergone a kind of natural process of preservation, which is described by Ruschenberger.-Also a small island in the Pacific, in S lat. 18° 35', W long. 70° 25'.
ARICA POINT, a cape of Peru, on the N part of the coast of Arica, in S lat. 18° 25'.
ARICAN, a town of Brazil, at the mouth of the Xingu, which runs into the Amazon.

ARICHAT, a town and port on the S side of Madame island, which is divided by Lennox passage from Cape Breton, opposite Cranberry island. Its harbour is well sheltered, and has depth of water for the largest ships. Pop. 2,000, principally Acadian French, who are engaged in the fisheries and coasting trade. Dry cod and pickled fish are exported from this place to Spain, Portugal, and the West Indies.
ARICIALE, a river of Naples, in Upper Calabria, falling into the gulf of Squillace, in N lat. 38° 42', E long. 16° 30′.
ARID ISLAND, an island in the Pacific, 27 m. W of Possession island, in N lat. 46° 30', E long. 41° 40'. It is high, and visible at a great distance.

ARIE'GE, a dep. in the south of France; formed out of the ancient Pays de Foix, the Couserans a district of Gascony, and some portions of the prov. of Languedoc; and bounded on the W and N by the dep. of Haute-Garonne; on the E by Aude; and on the S by the Pyrénées-Orientales. It is about 67 m. in length, and 48 m. in breadth; and contains an area of 454,808 hectares. The surface of this district is very unequal; a large portion of it is filled up by very high mountains, belonging to the Pyrenean system, barren rocks and narrow valleys. The highest points are the Maladetta 10,834 ft.; the Pique d' Estat 10,302 ft.; the Montcal, the Pic de Montoulion, the Pic de Serrère, the Pic de Fourcanade, the Montagne des Cuns, and the Montvallier. dwellings are dug in the rock itself. It is the see of The surface is divided into two valleys of nearly equal extent, the one that of the Salat, and the other that of the Ariège; but both belonging to the basin of the Garonne. The valley of the Salat forms the W portion of the dep., and is watered by numerous unimportant affluents of that river. The Gers, which drains the NE portion, falls into the Ariège beyond the frontiers of this dep. The Arize runs through the central part of the dep., between the two large valleys already mentioned, and joins the Garonne on the r. The climate is in general mild, notwithstanding the great elevation of the country; it is, however, more agreeable in the N than in the S, where, in consequence of the vicinity of the high mountains, the cold is excessive during winter and the heat intolerable in summer. The mineral riches are considerable. Immense quarries of marble, freestone, alabaster, plaster, and slate exist here; peat, coal, and asbestos are likewise tolerably abundant. There are also considerable mines of iron, lead, and copper. Gold, silver, torquoises, jet, and alum are met with.—The soil of A. presents every variety; the higher tracts afford good pasture, and furnish a considerable quantity of timber. The extent of forest-land is nearly 90,000 hectares. Potatoes are grown in large quantities, as the corn raised is scarcely adequate to the home consumption. In 1835, 17,685 hect. were under potatoes; and 28,650 under wheat. The lower parts, which are generally fertile, produce every kind of grain. The vines are of an inferior quality, the soil being in fact too rich for their successful cultivation; but about 12,000 hect. are under vines. The wild animals are bears, wild boars, wolves, foxes, badgers, roes, the chamois, the otter, the polecat, the hare, and the rabbit. Birds of prey and game are also common; and in the marshes the viper and some other species of the reptile tribe are not unfrequently met with. - The principal articles of export are iron, cork, resin, wool, corn, and cattle, together with mules which are much esteemed for their strength. In 1834, there were 53 iron-furnaces in this dep. There are also manufactories of hats, hosiery, cottons, woollens, linens, leather and paper. The pop. in 1801 was 196,454; in 1831, 253,121; in 1846, 265,607. Of this pop. in 1846 only the proportion of 34.94 per cent. above the age of 25 years could read and write. Ariège is divided into three arrondissements: viz., Foix, Pamiers, and Saint-Girons; which are subdivided into 20 cantons and 336 communes. It forms the dio. of the bishop of Pamiers. Its impôts directs amounted in 1839 to 862,076 francs.
ARIELLI, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Citra, 10 m. SE of Chieti. Pop. 1,200.
ARIEN, a small island in the Adriatic, 9 m. N of Venice, in N lat. 45° 45', E long. 13° 24'.

ARIENZO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Lavora, 12 m. NE of Capua, in N lat. 41°. Pop. 10,777. It consists of one well-built street, and has a pleasing appearance from the orange and fruitgardens which surround it. The Furcæ Caudinæ are supposed by many antiquarians to be the narrow defile between A. and the v. of Arpaia, 3 m. to the W.

ARIERO (Pico), a mountain in the island of Madeira, on the E side of the Coural, 3 hours ride from Funchal. Alt. 5,446 ft. above sea-level. Its horizontal distance from the Pico das Torrinhas is 5,490 ft.; from Pico Ruivo, 8,166 ft. Myrtles grow luxuriantly on the A. to an alt. of 3,000 ft. Vaccinia and the Juncus glaucus abound on the summit. The latter part of the ascent is along barren rocks of basalt and red tufa. - Bowdich.
ARIETORE, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 32 m. N of Tanjore, in N lat. 11° 11'.
